question: The boy ran away when he saw the dog. (away)
explanation: The word tells us where the boy ran. Words that show the location or direction of an action are adverbs of place.

question: I will call you later to discuss the plan. (later)
explanation: This word tells us when the call will happen. Words that show the time of an action are adverbs of time.

question: The girls sang beautifully during the concert. (beautifully)
explanation: This word describes how the girls sang. Words that show the way or manner an action is done are adverbs of manner.

question: I have been waiting for you ever since. (ever since)
explanation: The phrase describes the time of waiting. In this sentence it acts as an adverb to modify the verb.

question: The visitor left happily. (happily)
explanation: This word describes the way the visitor left. It is an adverb because it changes the meaning of a verb.

question: The questions are surprisingly easier than expected. (surprisingly)
explanation: This word tells us more about the adjective easier. Words that describe adjectives are called adverbs.

question: He seldom visits his home village. (seldom)
explanation: This word tells us how often he visits. Words that show how many times an action happens are adverbs of frequency.

question: I almost never finish my work on time. (almost)
explanation: This word shows the level or extent of the word never. Words that tell us how much are adverbs of degree.

question: Pegasus flew easily into the sky. (easily)
explanation: This word describes how Pegasus flew. It is an adverb because it gives more information about the action.

question: They rarely take lunch at noon do they? (rarely)
explanation: This word tells us how often they eat lunch. Because it describes the frequency of an action it is an adverb.

question: The rain fell gently on the crops. (gently)
explanation: This word describes how the rain fell. It is an adverb because it modifies the verb fell.

question: I cannot remember where he stays; I have been there only once. (once)
explanation: This word tells us how many times the person has been to that place. It acts as an adverb in this sentence.

question: The wind howled fiercely all night long. (fiercely)
explanation: This word describes the way the wind made noise. It is an adverb because it describes the action of howling.

question: The group decided to hold the meeting close to the office. (close)
explanation: This word tells us about the location of the meeting. In this context the word acts as an adverb.

question: Fortunately we found the missing keys. (Fortunately)
explanation: This word describes the whole situation in the sentence. These are known as sentence adverbs.

question: He was too tired to watch television. (too)
explanation: This word tells us the level or amount of being tired. It is an adverb of degree.

question: The teacher quietly advised the student. (quietly)
explanation: This word describes how the teacher gave advice. It is an adverb because it modifies the verb advised.

question: You speak English very well. (well)
explanation: This word describes how the person speaks. Because it tells us about the action of speaking it is an adverb.

question: He walked to and fro meditating. (to and fro)
explanation: This group of words tells us where he walked. A group of words acting as an adverb is an adverbial phrase.

question: It’s quite cold today. (quite)
explanation: This word tells us how cold it is. It describes the level of coldness so it is an adverb of degree.

question: The children always help their parents with house chores. (always)
explanation: This word tells us how often the children help. Words that show the frequency of an action are adverbs of frequency.

question: I Luckily remembered where my keys were. (Luckily)
explanation: This word describes how the person remembered. It is an adverb because it provides more detail about the action.

question: He was hardly ever late for his classes. (hardly ever)
explanation: This phrase shows the extent or level of being late. According to the source it is an adverb of degree.

question: She speaks very quietly. (very)
explanation: This word tells us the level or amount of quietness. It is an adverb of degree because it describes an adverb.

question: The doctor examines me monthly. (monthly)
explanation: This word tells us how often the exam happens. It is an adverb because it shows the frequency of the action.

question: You should not take these matters so lightly. (lightly)
explanation: This word describes how the matters should be taken. It is an adverb because it modifies the verb take.

question: Possibly he is mistaken. (Possibly)
explanation: This word describes the chance or possibility of the whole statement. This makes it a sentence adverb.

question: He jogged before breakfast. (before breakfast)
explanation: This group of words tells us when he jogged. It is an adverb phrase because it describes the time of an action.

question: Never was life more interesting. (Never)
explanation: This word tells us about the time of the situation. It is an adverb because it describes when things were interesting.

question: Everything happened quickly. (quickly)
explanation: This word describes how things happened. It is an adverb because it provides more information about the action.
